unit.subject: English
unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 5! This unit is all about action and movement — how people and animals move, how we describe fast and slow actions, and how we make good choices when we feel excited or under pressure. You will read about a runner who learns to accelerate, a horse that gallops across a field, and children who rescue a lost animal. You will meet strong verbs like squeeze, peek, proceed, and defend, and you will learn to say exactly *how* an action happens — quickly, slowly, or carefully. By the end, you will be ready to enjoy this unit's adventure story, “The Spiral Cave”.
unit.learningPath:
Start with the Vocabulary file. Learn a few action words each day and use them in sentences.
Work through the four mini-lessons in the Grammar file (past simple, adjective + noun, adverbs of manner, imperatives & modals).
Read the Story file, “The Spiral Cave”, and answer the questions.
Work through the four skills below: Reading & Listening → Speaking → Writing → Activities.
Finish with the Self-Assessment to see what you have learned.

visual.alt: Children using action words during active learning

rubrics.level4: Uses target words accurately and naturally

rubrics.level1: Capital letters and punctuation need support

outcomes.learningOutcome: Use strong action verbs to describe how people and animals move.
Tell a story about the past using the past simple tense (played, galloped, rescued, went).
Describe actions with adverbs of manner (quickly, slowly, carefully).
Build clear adjective + noun phrases (a greedy fox, a spiral staircase).
Give instructions with imperatives and use can and must correctly.
Read and understand an adventure story and talk about the choices the characters make.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Independent writing, presentation or project evidence

outcomes.learningOutcome: Write a short how-to guide and a narrative about an exciting event.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Independent writing, presentation or project evidence

readings.title: The Race at the Village Field
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript: On Saturday morning, the children met at the village field for the running race. Samira felt a lot of pressure, because everyone was watching. When the teacher blew his whistle, the runners began. At first Samira ran slowly. Then she started to accelerate. Her heart rate grew faster and faster. In the next field, a horse began to gallop beside the fence, as if it wanted to race too! Samira did not look back. She ran quickly but carefully, and she crossed the line first. “Well done,” said her teacher. “You stayed calm under pressure.”

readings.title: How Animals Move
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript: Animals move in many different ways. A horse can gallop across the grass. A snail moves slowly in a spiral shape. A cat will squeeze through a narrow gap. When a bird sees danger, it may give a signal to warn the others, and the whole group will fly away to prevent being caught. Some animals suffer when there is no water, so they must move far to find a river. Watching how animals move can excite us and teach us about the world.

readings.title: The Lost Goat (script)
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript: “Yesterday, Ishmael lost his little goat near the market. He began to gaze in every direction. ‘I must find her before dark,’ he said. He decided to check behind the shops. First he took a peek into the alley. Then he proceeded slowly. Suddenly he heard a soft sound. There was the goat! Ishmael ran to rescue her. ‘I will always defend you,’ he said, and he carried her home.”

readings.title: The Spiral Cave
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
One afternoon, in a quiet village near the forest, three children — Ibrahim, Isaac, and Musa — were playing beside the river. They often made up games and told stories, but today they found something strange. Behind a pile of rocks, there was a dark hole in the ground.
“Let me peek inside!” said Isaac.
Musa held his hand. “Be careful. It might not be safe.”
But Ibrahim had already taken a step forward. “It is dark, but I can see something. The floor goes down in a spiral.”
“Like a staircase?” asked Isaac.
“Yes! Let us explore it!” said Ibrahim, feeling excited.
The children looked around for a strong stick and a torch. “We must check that it is safe,” said Musa. “We do not want to fall.”
They went in slowly. “First, peek inside. Then, proceed carefully,” whispered Ibrahim, copying what their teacher always said in science class.
Inside, the air was cold. The spiral tunnel turned and turned. Isaac pointed to the wall. “Look! A drawing of people… and a lion?”
They kept walking until they reached a wider space. “It is a cave!” shouted Musa. Their voices echoed off the stone.
Suddenly, they heard a soft sound.
“Was that a whistle?” asked Ibrahim.
“No,” said Isaac. “That was an animal!”
Then, out of the shadows, two eyes appeared. A small dog — thin and shivering — stepped forward. It wagged its tail.
“It looks hungry,” said Musa. “We have to rescue it!”
Ibrahim reached into his bag and pulled out a piece of bread. He called gently to the dog. “Come here, boy.”
The dog sniffed and walked closer. “I think he is friendly,” said Isaac. “He does not want to hurt us.”
“We should give him a name,” said Ibrahim.
“Not yet,” said Musa. “First we need to check that he is not sick.”
The children carried the dog out of the cave. Outside, the sun was low in the sky.
“We must accelerate,” said Isaac. “It is getting late!”
Back in the village, they went to the animal helper, Mama Hannah. She was kind and wise.
“Where did you find him?” she asked.
“In a spiral tunnel near the river,” said Ibrahim.
“You were lucky,” said Mama Hannah. “That tunnel is very old. There is pressure from the soil above it. It could fall in at any time.”
“We did not know,” said Musa. “We were too excited.”
“Next time you must tell your parents first,” she said. “But I am glad you brought him to me.”
The dog was given clean water and food. He wagged his tail and let Musa squeeze his paw gently.
“He is strong,” said Mama Hannah. “He only needs rest.”
“But who left him there?” asked Isaac.
“We may never know,” said Mama Hannah. “Sometimes people are greedy. They want an animal but do not care for it. That is not right. An animal like this can suffer when no one looks after it.”
The children were quiet. They did not like the thought of anyone being so unkind.
“We can help him,” said Musa. “Let us make posters and describe him. Someone might be looking for him.”
“That is a kind plan,” said Mama Hannah.
That night, Ibrahim wrote in his notebook:
“We found a cave, a spiral path, and a small dog. We learned to move slowly and to think. We felt pressure and fear, but we stayed strong.”
The next day, the children shared their story at school. Their teacher smiled. “You followed instructions, stayed calm, and made good choices. That is something to be proud of.”
“What should we name him now?” asked Isaac.
“How about the rescued dog?” said Musa. “He is strong, like a lion.”
“Perfect,” said Ibrahim. “He is our little rescued lion.”
the rescued dog barked — happy, and safe at last.

grammar.title: Past Simple Tense
grammar.explanation: The past simple tense is used to talk about actions that already happened and are finished. It lets us tell stories about the past — what we did yesterday or last week. For example: “She rescued the bird yesterday.”  “They galloped across the field.”
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Regular verbs: add -ed to the base verb.  play → played, check → checked, gallop → galloped.
Irregular verbs: these change in their own way.  go → went, run → ran, find → found, eat → ate.
The pattern is: Subject + past verb + (when).  “We found a cave last Saturday.”
To make it negative, use did not + base verb:  “She did not rescue the bird.”
To ask a question, use Did + subject + base verb:  “Did she rescue the bird?”
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “Yesterday she rescue the bird.”  ✗   (base verb, no ending)
Fix: Add -ed for the past: “Yesterday she rescued the bird.”  ✓
Also watch irregulars — “I goed” ✗ becomes “I went” ✓.
grammar.memoryTip: Say to yourself: “Yesterday is history!” If it already happened, the verb changes — usually by adding -ed.
grammar.practice: Write the correct past simple form of the verb in brackets. | They ______ (play) in the park last Sunday. | She ______ (find) a lost dog on the way home. | We ______ (check) the tunnel before we went in. | He ______ (go) to the market after school. | The horse ______ (gallop) across the field.

grammar.title: Adjective + Noun Phrases
grammar.explanation: An adjective is a word that describes something. A noun is a word that names a person, place, or thing. When we put an adjective before a noun, we make a clearer, more interesting phrase. For example: “a greedy fox”, “a spiral staircase”.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Choose an adjective (a describing word): greedy, spiral, hungry, shiny, tall.
Choose a noun (a naming word): fox, staircase, dog, car, tree.
Put the adjective before the noun:  a greedy fox,  a spiral staircase,  a shiny car.
If you use two adjectives, keep it simple: “a big blue ball”, not a long list.
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “a fox greedy”.  ✗   (adjective after the noun)
Fix: In English the adjective goes first: “a greedy fox”.  ✓
grammar.memoryTip: Adjectives are helpers that go in front. Think: “Describe it first, then name it — greedy fox!”
grammar.practice: Add an adjective to complete each phrase. (One good answer is given as a clue.) | a ______ staircase   (clue: goes round and round) | a ______ dog   (clue: it has not eaten) | a ______ race   (clue: it makes you cheer) | a ______ fox   (clue: it wants everything)

grammar.title: Adverbs of Manner
grammar.explanation: An adverb of manner tells us how an action happens. Since this unit is all about action and movement, these words make your sentences come alive. For example: “The horse galloped quickly.”  “We walked carefully into the cave.”
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Most adverbs of manner are made by adding -ly to an adjective.  slow → slowly, quick → quickly, quiet → quietly.
If the adjective ends in -y, change y to i and add -ly.  happy → happily, easy → easily.
The adverb usually comes after the verb:  “She ran quickly.”  “He spoke softly.”
A few are irregular: good → well.  “He plays football well.”
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “The snail moved slow.”  ✗   (that is an adjective)
Fix: Use the -ly adverb to say how: “The snail moved slowly.”  ✓
grammar.memoryTip: If the word answers “How?”, it usually ends in -ly. How did she run? Quickly!
grammar.practice: Change the adjective in brackets into an adverb of manner to finish the sentence. | The children walked ______ (careful) into the dark tunnel. | The car moved ______ (quick) down the road. | She opened the gift ______ (quiet) so it would stay a surprise. | The old man spoke ______ (slow) and kindly.

grammar.title: Imperatives and Modal Verbs (can / must)
grammar.explanation: An imperative gives an instruction or a command — perfect for how-to guides. A modal verb like can or must tells us what is possible or what is important. For example: “Check your work!”  “You must be careful.”  “Birds can whistle tunes.”
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Imperative: start with the base verb, with no subject.  “Proceed carefully.”  “Peek inside.”
To say “do not”, add don't:  “Don't run near the cave.”
can = able to / possible:  “A horse can gallop.”  Keep the base verb after it: can gallop, not can gallops.
must = it is important / a rule:  “You must check that it is safe.”
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “You must to check the cave.”  ✗   Also: “A bird can whistles.”  ✗
Fix: No “to”, and keep the base verb: “You must check the cave.”  ✓   “A bird can whistle.”  ✓
grammar.memoryTip: After can and must, the verb stays in its plain base form — “can go”, “must stop”. And an imperative jumps straight to the action: “No ‘you’, just the verb!”
grammar.practice: Choose the correct word or form in brackets. | ______ (Check / Checks) your answers before you finish.  (make it a command) | A horse can ______ (gallop / gallops) very fast. | You must ______ (proceed / to proceed) carefully in the dark. | ______ (Don't / Not) touch the wet paint!

grammar.title: Grade 4 Editing and Application
grammar.explanation: Strong writers apply unit grammar while revising complete paragraphs for meaning, accuracy and flow.
grammar.ruleAndExamples: Read a paragraph aloud. Check sentence completeness, agreement, tense, joining words and punctuation. Revise one feature at a time.
grammar.commonMistake: Mistake: changing words without checking whether the paragraph still means the same thing. Fix: explain the purpose of every revision.
grammar.practice: Edit a six-sentence paragraph using the unit language focus. Mark four changes and explain how two changes improve clarity.

grammar.title: Grade 4 Editing and Application
grammar.explanation: Strong writers apply unit grammar while revising complete paragraphs for meaning, accuracy and flow.

speaking.title: Speaking 1: Describe an Action
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Choose an action and describe it using an adverb of manner (a word that says *how*). Practise saying:
“The horse galloped quickly across the field.”
“The snail moved slowly up the wall.”
“I opened the door carefully so it would not make a noise.”
Now make three sentences of your own about actions you did today. Record yourself and listen back.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise describe an action and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

speaking.title: Speaking 2: Tell About a Time You Felt Pressure
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Talk for one minute about a time you felt pressure or excitement — maybe a race, a test, or reciting the Qur'an in front of others. Try these sentence starters:
“One day, I had to ______.”
“I felt ______ because ______.”
“First I ______. Then I ______.”
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise tell about a time you felt pressure and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

speaking.title: Speaking 3: Act and Guess
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Play with your AI tutor, a friend, or your family:
One person acts out an action word from the Vocabulary list (squeeze, gallop, peek, whistle, gaze).
The other person guesses: “Are you ______ing?”
Answer: “Yes, I am!” or “No, try again.” Then swap turns.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise act and guess and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

speaking.title: Speaking 4: Vocabulary Expert Interview
speaking.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.2 curriculum expansion
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Choose six unit words. Ask and answer a clear question about each word using complete sentences.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise vocabulary expert interview and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

speaking.title: Speaking 5: Explain and Respond
speaking.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.2 curriculum expansion
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Explain one important unit idea, give a reason with because, and respond to two follow-up questions.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise explain and respond and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

speaking.title: Speaking 6: Three-Minute Unit Talk
speaking.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.2 curriculum expansion
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Plan and record a three-minute talk using an opening, three connected ideas with evidence or examples, and a purposeful closing.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise three-minute unit talk and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

writing.title: Writing 1: How-To Guide: How to Rescue a Lost Animal
writing.promptAndInstructions:
Write a short how-to guide with 4–5 steps. Use imperatives (Check…, Move…, Proceed…) and sequencing words (First, Then, After that, Finally). For example:
First, move slowly so you do not frighten the animal. Then, check that it is not hurt…
writing.modelText: A strong Grade 4 response uses organised paragraphs, precise unit words, relevant evidence or examples, and a purposeful conclusion about how-to guide: how to rescue a lost animal.
writing.successCriteria: I answered the prompt; organised ideas into paragraphs; used precise Unit vocabulary; supported ideas with details or evidence; checked cohesion and punctuation; revised my work
writing.support: Plan a topic sentence and supporting details, then use the word bank and a source model.
writing.extension: Vary sentence openings, compare two ideas, and strengthen the conclusion.

writing.title: Writing 2: Narrative: An Exciting Adventure
writing.promptAndInstructions: Write a short story (6–10 sentences) about an exciting adventure where someone moves quickly and makes a good choice. Use the past simple tense all the way through. Try to include at least five words from your Vocabulary list, and underline them.
writing.modelText: A strong Grade 4 response uses organised paragraphs, precise unit words, relevant evidence or examples, and a purposeful conclusion about narrative: an exciting adventure.
writing.successCriteria: I answered the prompt; organised ideas into paragraphs; used precise Unit vocabulary; supported ideas with details or evidence; checked cohesion and punctuation; revised my work
writing.support: Plan a topic sentence and supporting details, then use the word bank and a source model.

activities.title: Activities 1: Match the Action
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Match each verb to its meaning. Write the pairs (for example: 1 - c).
gallop
peek
rescue
accelerate
a. to look quickly or secretly
b. to save someone from danger
c. to run fast like a horse
d. to go faster
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 2: Past or Now?
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Write P for past or N for now (present) for each sentence.
The horse gallops every morning.
Yesterday she rescued a bird.
They checked the cave carefully.
I squeeze the orange each day.
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 3: Add the Adverb
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Add an adverb of manner (quickly, slowly, carefully, quietly) to finish each sentence.
The children walked ______ into the dark tunnel.
The car moved ______ down the empty road.
She opened the gift ______ so it would stay a surprise.
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 4: Build a Phrase
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Put an adjective before each noun to make a phrase, like “a greedy fox”.
a ______ staircase
a ______ dog
a ______ race
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 5: Vocabulary Sort and Explain
activities.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.2 curriculum expansion
activities.instructionsAndItems: Sort twelve unit words into useful categories. Explain the rule for each category in a complete sentence.
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 6: Reading Evidence Hunt
activities.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.2 curriculum expansion
activities.instructionsAndItems: Find six important details in the unit texts. Record the detail and explain what it helps the reader understand.
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

assignments.title: Action and Movement learning portfolio
assignments.instructions: Submit Writing 1: How-To Guide: How to Rescue a Lost Animal and a recording of Speaking 1: Describe an Action. Improve both pieces using the Grade 4 rubrics.
assignments.sourceFile: Derived from unit writing and speaking tasks

quizzes.question: What does 'gallop' mean?
quizzes.options: A shape that curls round and round, moving out from the centre. | How fast something happens, or how much of it there is. | To go forward or to keep going. | To run fast on four legs, like a horse.
quizzes.correctAnswer: To run fast on four legs, like a horse.
quizzes.explanation: The horse began to gallop across the field.

quizzes.question: What does 'accelerate' mean?
quizzes.options: A shape that curls round and round, moving out from the centre. | How fast something happens, or how much of it there is. | To go faster, or to make something go faster. | To look at something quickly or secretly.
quizzes.correctAnswer: To go faster, or to make something go faster.
quizzes.explanation: The car can accelerate when the driver presses the pedal.

quizzes.question: What does 'proceed' mean?
quizzes.options: How fast something happens, or how much of it there is. | To go forward or to keep going. | To look at something for a long time. | To look at something quickly or secretly.
quizzes.correctAnswer: To go forward or to keep going.
quizzes.explanation: After the bell rings, we will proceed to the playground.

quizzes.question: What does 'spiral' mean?
quizzes.options: A shape that curls round and round, moving out from the centre. | To look at something for a long time. | To look at something quickly or secretly. | To look at something to make sure it is correct or safe.
quizzes.correctAnswer: A shape that curls round and round, moving out from the centre.
quizzes.explanation: The snail moved in a spiral on the ground.

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'The Race at the Village Field': In the next field, a horse began to _____ beside the fence, as if it wanted to race too!
quizzes.explanation: The text says: In the next field, a horse began to gallop beside the fence, as if it wanted to race too!
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'The Spiral Cave': Then, _____ carefully,” whispered Ibrahim, copying what their teacher always said in science class.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: Then, proceed carefully,” whispered Ibrahim, copying what their teacher always said in science class.
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'How Animals Move': A snail moves slowly in a _____ shape.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: A snail moves slowly in a spiral shape.
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'The Race at the Village Field': Her heart _____ grew faster and faster.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: Her heart rate grew faster and faster.
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'The Lost Goat (script)': First he took a _____ into the alley.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: First he took a peek into the alley.
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'The Lost Goat (script)': He began to _____ in every direction.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: He began to gaze in every direction.
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

teacherNotes.note: Model new language aloud, check meaning before independent work, use mixed-response modes, and give specific feedback on complete sentences, text evidence and vocabulary precision.

💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you read ‘The Lost Goat’ aloud slowly, then ask me questions about it?”
“Can you play a listening game where I have to spot the action verbs?”
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you help me practise describing actions with -ly words?”
“Can you play ‘Act and Guess’ with the action verbs?”
“Can you listen to my story about feeling pressure and help me improve it?”
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you check my how-to guide and tell me if my steps are clear?”
“Can you check that I used the past tense correctly in my story?”
“Can you help me add adverbs to make my writing more interesting?”
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you quiz me on these five moving words?”
“What is the difference between accelerate and proceed?”
“Can you give me one more example sentence for each word?”
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“What is the difference between peek and gaze?”
“Can you play a describing game with me?”
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you use pressure and suffer in new sentences for me?”
“How can I stay calm when I feel pressure?”
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Why is it good to admit when we make a mistake?”
“Can you give me a short story that uses rescue, defend and greedy?”
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you check my grammar answers?”
“Can you give me five more sentences to change into the past tense?”
“Why do we say ‘she galloped’ but ‘she did not gallop’?”

answerKey.answerOrGuidance: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.
answerKey.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.2 curriculum expansion

answerKey.answerOrGuidance: To run fast on four legs, like a horse. The horse began to gallop across the field.
To go faster, or to make something go faster. The car can accelerate when the driver presses the pedal.
To go forward or to keep going. After the bell rings, we will proceed to the playground.
A shape that curls round and round, moving out from the centre. The snail moved in a spiral on the ground.
How fast something happens, or how much of it there is. Your heart rate goes up when you run.
To look at something quickly or secretly. I took a peek at the surprise gift before my birthday.
To look at something for a long time. She likes to gaze at the stars in the night sky.
To look at something to make sure it is correct or safe. Please check your homework before you turn it in.
A sign or message that tells you something. The green light is a signal for us to go.
To tell someone what something is like. Can you describe your favourite animal to me?

selfAssessment.statement: I can make adjective + noun phrases.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can give instructions with imperatives and use can and must.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can understand the story and talk about the characters' choices.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself
